title: Vertical Shuttling Protocols for Trapped Ions in Multi-Rail, Multi-Zone Surface Ion Trap Architectures
abstract: We investigate optimized vertical ion-shuttling protocols for trapped-ion applications across a range of ion-trap experiments, including three-dimensional gradient-measurement sensors, on-chip ion fluorescence collection and imaging, improved laser accessibility, and quantum information processing. In this work, we focus on minimizing motional energy gain during ion transport. Our findings indicate that anomalous heating becomes the dominant limiting factor only for shuttling durations exceeding $500~μ\mathrm{s}$, whereas the final motional excitation is strongly dependent on the selected transport protocol. Using a recently measured heating rate of $(3.1 \pm 0.35)$ quanta/ms at an ion--surface separation of $134 \pm 1.5~μ\mathrm{m}$, we demonstrate that the motional excitation can be restricted to fewer than eight quanta when the ion is vertically displaced by $86~μ\mathrm{m}$ from its initial position. These results enable adiabatic shuttling within $0.5~\mathrm{ms}$, thereby meeting the operational requirements for high-fidelity quantum sensing and coherent control.
summary: This paper presents an optimized method for vertically shuttling ions in a multi-zone surface trap. By using a specific hyperbolic tangent voltage ramp, the researchers found they could move ions vertically while keeping motional heating extremely low. The study balances the need for fast transport to avoid anomalous heating with the need for smooth movement to avoid kinetic energy gain. This protocol is crucial for maintaining the high-fidelity states required for quantum computing and sensing.
main_problem: Optimizing vertical ion-shuttling protocols in multi-rail surface ion traps to minimize motional energy gain from both the transport process and anomalous heating.
main_result: The authors identified an optimal hyperbolic tangent (tanh) voltage ramp with a smoothness parameter N=2.5, demonstrating that vertical displacement of 86 um can be achieved with motional excitation limited to fewer than 8 quanta.
method: The study uses electrostatic modeling with a gapless-plane approximation and compares different voltage trajectories (linear, sinusoidal, and tanh) to minimize kinetic energy gain and heating.
model_or_system: A microfabricated multi-rail, multi-zone surface-electrode ion trap featuring RF electrodes for radial confinement and segmented DC electrodes for axial confinement.
key_observables: Motional energy gain (mean motional quanta), kinetic energy gain, radial and axial secular frequencies, and anomalous heating rate.
important_parameters: Shuttling duration (0.45-0.5 ms), smoothness parameter N, ion-surface separation (86 to 134 um), and heating rate (3.1 quanta/ms).
main_assumptions: Employs the gapless-plane approximation for electrode modeling and assumes anomalous heating scales as 1/h^4.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the trap design; Figure 2 illustrates the pseudopotential and local minima; Figure 3 shows the shift in trapping potential; Figure 7 illustrates the voltage ramp and ion displacement; Figure 8 shows trap parameter variations; Figure 9 compares kinetic energy gain for different N values.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the multi-rail trap architecture, describes the electrostatic and pseudopotential modeling, evaluates different shuttling trajectories, analyzes the impact of the smoothness parameter N and duration T, and concludes with an optimized protocol for quantum sensing.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in quantum information and AMO physics as it provides a practical framework for high-fidelity control and transport of ions, which is essential for scalable quantum computing and sensing architectures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Step by step multi-rail trap design (a) Single region trap with RF electrodes of width ‘b=c’ and ground (control) electrode ‘a’ in between, (b) Two trapping region (c) Set of segmented DC electrodes to control ion in z-direction (d) single trapping zone using RF and segemented DC electrodes (e) Dual trapping zones using multi rf electordes (f) Scalable four trapping zones with RF and control electrodes.
Figure 2. (a) Top view of the optimized multi region trap design. (b) Illustrates the pseudo potential along with the local minima generated due to applied VRF to the RF electrodes

Figure 3. Behavior of the trapping regions when an RF voltage 𝑽𝐑𝐅is applied to the central electrode. The red circle indicates the initial trapping region where ions can be confined. (a) Variation in the heights of two trapping regions as a function of the applied 𝑽𝐑𝐅 . (b) single trapping region illustrating the vertical displacement of the ion position achieved by gradually

Figure 4. Variation in Trap parameters changed during shuttling in time ‘t’. (a) Change of voltages on the central electrode ‘VCE’. (b) Deviation in ion‘s position (c) Change of radial secular frequency from 1.55MHz to 2.4MHz.
Figure 5. Plot shows significant increase in trap depth upon decrease in ion height. The Ion height has been changed by applying VRF on the central electrode.

Fig. 5 (c) shows a clear increase in the radial frequency as the ion is vertically shuttled over the time interval 𝑡. This increase is attributed to the enhanced confinement arising from the applied DC electrode potential. Despite this change, the secular frequency remains within an acceptable operating range and does not introduce any adverse effects on the trapping performance or experimental stability.
Figure 6. Variation in voltages applied on central electrode depends strongly on the N parameter. Smaller N leads to linear trajectory.

Figure 7. Ion shuttling profile with 1ms time scale and N=10. (a) when voltage was increased from 0V to 100V on central RF electrode during 1ms (b) the ion is decreased from 134µm to 86µm, (c) the gain of K.E as the ion will experience a kick during shuttling so the gain is maximum (d) overlap of (a and c) show sharp rise of energy gain at voltage change duration.

Figure 8. The maximum motional excitation induced during transport. Zoomed in parts show secular frequencies during shuttling while there is a gain in Kinetic energy of ion. (a) Radial frequency at ~2.2MHz (b) axial frequency at 300kHz (c) Total secular frequency with beats show axial while fluctuations refer to the radial frequency.
Figure 9. Comparison of gain in kinetic energy as a function of time ‘t’ with different values of N parameters. It could be observed that N=2.5 gives less gain in motional energy.
